title: Companies are drowning in data
content: Most companies now collect enormous amounts of data: clicks, purchases, sensor readings, and customer interactions. The hard part is often no longer collection itself. The harder task is turning raw information into something reliable, consistent, and useful for real decisions.
next: Where does all of this data even come from?

title: Data pours in from everywhere
content: A single online order can generate dozens of records: the page views before it, the items added to a cart, the payment, the shipping updates, and the support chat afterward. Multiply that by millions of customers and thousands of products, and the scale grows very quickly. One business event often becomes many data events spread across many systems.
next: If there's so much of it, isn't storing it the hard part?

title: Storage stopped being the problem
content: Twenty years ago, keeping large datasets was expensive for many companies. Today, cloud storage is much cheaper, so organizations can keep far more historical data. The bottleneck shifted: the harder question is often not 'can we store it?' but 'can we trust it and make sense of it?'
next: So what makes raw data so hard to use?

title: Raw data is messy
content: Data arrives in different formats, often full of duplicates, gaps, and errors. One system spells a country 'USA', another 'United States'. Timestamps may use different time zones, and customer IDs may not line up cleanly across tools. Before anyone can trust a number, the data has to be cleaned, matched, standardized, and organized.
next: Who actually needs all this cleaned-up data?

title: Everyone wants something different
content: An analyst wants a tidy table for a weekly report. A data scientist wants detailed records to train a model. An executive wants a single dashboard number. A live app wants an instant answer. The same underlying data has to serve very different needs without producing contradictory results.
next: What happens when everyone keeps their own copy?

title: Copies create chaos
content: When each team keeps its own copy of data, those copies drift apart over time. One report may count refunds one way, while another report counts them differently. Then teams stop trusting both numbers. This is why organizations aim for a governed shared data layer - often called a single source of truth.
next: How does data become trustworthy in the first place?

title: Data takes a journey
content: Useful data is usually the result of a pipeline. Raw information is collected, stored, validated, cleaned, modeled, and then served to people and systems that need it. Each step adds quality, context, lineage, and trust. In practice, much of the value in data work is created along this path, not at the moment of collection.
next: Why can't a normal database just handle all of this?

title: Scale changes everything
content: A spreadsheet works well for small datasets. At very large scale, tools designed for local files become slow, fragile, or impossible to manage. Systems that handle small data well can struggle when data volume, user concurrency, and update frequency all rise together. Large-scale problems usually require distributed tools and different design choices.
next: And what about questions that can't wait?

title: Speed matters too
content: Some questions can wait for an overnight report. Others - fraud detection, live recommendations, or a delivery map - need answers in seconds or even milliseconds. A modern data system often has to support both slow batch analysis and fast operational response.
next: Is there a name for the work of solving all this?

title: This work is data engineering
content: Data engineering is the discipline of building systems that turn raw, messy data into clean, reliable, ready-to-use datasets at scale. It supports dashboards, analytics, applications, and AI models by making data dependable, documented, and accessible to the people and systems that need it.
next: Why was this so hard with the tools companies used first?

title: Why this story matters
content: Over the next modules we'll trace how the tools evolved: from rigid databases, to the big-data era, to Apache Spark, and finally to platforms like Databricks. Each step was an answer to a limit of the one before it.
next: It starts with the tool companies reached for first: the traditional database.
